coredata.dc:title: A 3D porous structured cellulose nanofibrils-based hydrogel with carbon dots-enhanced synergetic effects of adsorption and photocatalysis for effective Cr(VI) removal
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Dong
Huang, Yong
Ma, Qiang
Qiao, Jianzheng
Guo, Xin
Wu, Yiqiang
coredata.dc:description: Increasing chromium pollution poses a significant threat to the global environment and human health, and it is critical to remove hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) from water. Hence, a three-dimensional (3D) porous structured cellulose nanofibrils-based hydrogel (CNH) was synthesised using cellulose nanofibrils (CNs), carbon dots (CDs) and α-FeOOH, with CDs-enhanced synergetic effects of adsorption and photocatalysis for removing Cr(VI). In the CNH, CNs were conducted as a natural frame to construct a 3D porous structure increasing the specific surface area and providing additional active sites, CDs enhanced the amino group-induced adsorption for Cr(VI) and facilitated the transfer of photogenerated electron from α-FeOOH, and α-FeOOH was nanosized photocatalyst for generating photogenerated electron. A series of adsorption and photocatalysis experiments showed the superior adsorption-photocatalytic performance, as demonstrated by the large adsorption capacity and high kinetic constant of 115.98 mg/g and 0.06380, respectively, which was about 8 and 26 times as high as that of α-FeOOH. This superior removal performance was due to the mechanism of CDs-enhanced adsorption-photocatalysis synergy. This study not only provided a novel strategy for preparing green and efficient bifunctional materials for high-efficient Cr(VI) removal but also proposed a corresponding novel mechanism for CDs-enhanced synergetic effect of adsorption and photocatalysis.

Mechanism for CDs-enhanced synergetic effect of adsorption and photocatalysis.

head.ce:title: A 3D porous structured cellulose nanofibrils-based hydrogel with carbon dots-enhanced synergetic effects of adsorption and photocatalysis for effective Cr(VI) removal

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Increasing chromium pollution poses a significant threat to the global environment and human health, and it is critical to remove hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) from water. Hence, a three-dimensional (3D) porous structured cellulose nanofibrils-based hydrogel (CNH) was synthesised using cellulose nanofibrils (CNs), carbon dots (CDs) and α-FeOOH, with CDs-enhanced synergetic effects of adsorption and photocatalysis for removing Cr(VI). In the CNH, CNs were conducted as a natural frame to construct a 3D porous structure increasing the specific surface area and providing additional active sites, CDs enhanced the amino group-induced adsorption for Cr(VI) and facilitated the transfer of photogenerated electron from α-FeOOH, and α-FeOOH was nanosized photocatalyst for generating photogenerated electron. A series of adsorption and photocatalysis experiments showed the superior adsorption-photocatalytic performance, as demonstrated by the large adsorption capacity and high kinetic constant of 115.98 mg/g and 0.06380, respectively, which was about 8 and 26 times as high as that of α-FeOOH. This superior removal performance was due to the mechanism of CDs-enhanced adsorption-photocatalysis synergy. This study not only provided a novel strategy for preparing green and efficient bifunctional materials for high-efficient Cr(VI) removal but also proposed a corresponding novel mechanism for CDs-enhanced synergetic effect of adsorption and photocatalysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The growing threat of chromium pollution to the global environment and human health necessitates the removal of Cr(VI) from wastewater. Many treatment methods have been introduced to remove Cr(VI) ions. Among them, adsorption and photocatalysis are the two most important treatment methods with outstanding advantages of simplicity, safety and efficiency, but both have certain shortcomings and limitations [1]. The adsorption method can achieve enrichment and transformation of Cr(VI) ions, while the photocatalysis method can effectively reduce the toxicity of Cr(VI) ions [2]. The integration of efficient adsorption and photocatalytic reduction into the design of novel multifunctional materials is expected to significantly improve the removal efficiency of Cr(VI) ions.

Recently, the use of cellulose-based hydrogels for Cr(VI) ion removal has attracted significant attention. Wang et al. synthesised cellulose hydrogel-coated nanometer zero-valent iron-intercalated montmorillonite and demonstrated its Cr(VI) removal efficiency of 98 % [3]. Song et al. synthesised a carboxymethyl cellulose/polyethylenimine hydrogel using a facile one-step method to improve Cr(VI) removal and confirmed that this hydrogel could be successfully regenerated and reused [4]. Peng et al. prepared a cellulose nanofibrils-based hydrogel, with its maximum sorption capacity for Cr(VI) reaching 648.4 mg/g [5]. Based on these studies, cellulose-based hydrogels have significant potential in removing Cr(VI) ions.

As an important photocatalyst, alpha ferric hydroxide (α-FeOOH) has been extensively studied in recent years. The α-FeOOH is widely distributed in water, soil and rocks on the earth's surface and has the advantages of safety, nontoxicity, corrosion resistance and low cost [6]. Furthermore, the α-FeOOH has lots of reactive hydroxyl sites and exhibits excellent conductivity on the surface. As a result, it has been used for treating organic pollutants and heavy metals [7]. However, at the nano level, the α-FeOOH can easily agglomerate, which significantly degrades its adsorption performance and photocatalytic activity [8]. However, suitable support can solve this drawback. Hence, the design and preparation of suitable support for α-FeOOH is crucial to its application.

Carbon dots (CDs) have piqued increasing interest because of their unique properties [9]. The CDs can be used to improve adsorption and photocatalysis [10]. Luo et al. synthesised a novel 3D porous fluorescent hydrogel using amino-modified CDs and cellulose nanofibres and then confirmed that the CDs could provide many binding sites for Cr(VI) ions [11]. Habibi-Yangjeh et al. prepared a series of g-C3N4 nanosheet/CDs/Ag2CO3 photocatalysts and demonstrated that the improved degradation ability was related to CDs acting as electron mediators [12]. Hence, CDs seem to be promising nanomaterials for improving Cr(VI) adsorption and photocatalysis.

In this work, an adsorptive photocatalyst, namely, cellulose nanofibrils-based hydrogel (CNH) was first synthesised, in which the CNs offered the “skeleton” of the hydrogel to build the 3D porous structure, CDs enhanced the amino group-induced adsorption for Cr(VI) and accelerated the transportation of photogenerated electron, and α-FeOOH was nanophotocatalyst for generating photogenerated electron. The morphology, structure and photoluminescence (PL) features of the novel CNH were characterised. Then, the adsorptive and photocatalytic activities of CNH in Cr(VI) removal was investigated. On this basis, many experiments were conducted to reveal CDs-enhanced synergistic mechanism of CNH.
Materials and reagents were included in Supplementary material.

The CNs preparation procedures were similar to those reported in the literature [13]. Bleached wood pulp (10 g) was added to deionised water (300 mL) with vigorous stirring. NaBr (0.4 g) and 2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO) radical (0.08 g) were then added. A NaOCl solution (60 g, 10 wt%) was added dropwise at room temperature. Subsequently, the pH of the mixture was adjusted to 10 by adding a NaOH solution (1 M). The oxidation time was 6 h. The mixture was then washed to neutral and homogenised using an M110P microfluidiser. After five high-pressure homogenisation cycles, the mixture was placed in regenerated cellulose dialysis tubes and dialysed against deionised water for five days. The resulting CNs suspension with a concentration of 0.5 wt% was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C until its final use. The CNs' carboxyl content was measured to be 0.67 mmol/g.

Ethylenediamine (EDA, 5.36 mL) and citric acid (CA, 4.2 g) were mixed with deionised water (100 mL). The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave chamber. The chamber was sealed and placed in an oven at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling to 25 °C, a dark brown supernatant was obtained, which was then placed in regenerated cellulose dialysis tubes and dialysed against deionised water for five days. The concentration was set to 0.1 wt% by adding the appropriate amount of deionised water.

CDs was conjugated on CNs according to previous studies with some modifications [14]. First, the CNs suspension (20 g, 0.5 wt%) was stirred continuously at 35 °C for 4 h. Subsequently, an acetate buffer (5 mL, pH = 3.5), N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N’-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 0.06 g), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 0.23 g) and a CDs solution (15 g, 0.1 wt%) were added with continuous stirring. The mixture was stirred at 25 °C for 12 h. The product was purified through a dialysis bag in deionised water for five days. Finally, the target product concentration was diluted to 0.1 wt%, and called as CNs–CDs.

In the experiment, α-FeOOH was synthesised using a solvothermal method [15]. An FeSO4·7H2O powder (2.1 g) was added to 63-mL deionised water. Then, a H2O2 solution (18 mL, 30 %) was added with constant stirring. The mixture was well combined and reacted at 150 °C for 6 h. Precipitates were obtained and washed with deionised water. Next, the obtained α-FeOOH powder was dried at 80 °C for 5 h.

First, acrylic acid (AA, 2.0 g) was mixed with a NaOH solution (4.72 mL, 5 mol/L). CNs–CDs (1.8 g), N’,N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA, 0.02 g), potassium persulfate (PPS, 0.024 g) and a specific amount of α-FeOOH were then added with continuous stirring under a nitrogen atmosphere. The mixture was heated to 40 °C and maintained at that temperature for 6 h to obtain a hydrogel. This hydrogel was submerged in water for two days to remove the residual reagents. Based on the amounts of α-FeOOH (0.05, 0.10, 0.15 and 0.20 g), the obtained hydrogel was labelled CNH-1, CNH-2, CNH-3 and CNH-4, respectively. Meanwhile, the controlled hydrogel was prepared using CNs (1.8 g) instead of CNs–CDs (1.8 g). The mass of α-FeOOH was chosen to be 0.15 g, and the controlled hydrogel was labelled as CH-3.

Adsorption experiments were conducted in the dark to investigate the adsorption for Cr(VI) removal. The prepared samples (0.10 g) and a Cr(VI) ion solution (200 mL) were added to a 250 mL flask. The flask was then placed in a thermostatic shaker at a preset temperature (25 °C) and constant speed (150 rpm). All these sorption experiments were performed three times. The effects of different factors were examined in these adsorption experiments: various Cr(VI) concentrations (10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 mg/L) at a constant contact time (one day) and different contact times (10, 20, 30, 40, 50 and 60 min) at a constant initial concentration (20 mg/L). The concentration was determined using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer from its 1,5-diphenylcarbazide complex at 540 nm.

The adsorption and photocatalytic experiments were conducted using a self-made reactor equipped with a 500 W high-pressure mercury lamp as described in previous references [16]. First, the prepared sample was added to a 500-mL Cr(VI) solution. The solution was stirred in the dark for 60 min to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Second, the solution was stirred under UV irradiation from the high-pressure mercury lamp. At a certain time interval of 20 min, 5 mL of the solution was extracted and filtrated through a 0.45 μm filter membrane. The whole adsorption and photocatalytic process lasted for 140 min.

A schematic illustration of CNH synthesis is depicted in Fig. 1 . CNs, CDs and α-FeOOH were three kinds of main materials. First, a condensation reaction was performed to form CNs–CDs based on the raw material CNs and CDs. Then, the skeletal material CNs–CDs, the monomer AA, the photocatalyst α-FeOOH, and the crosslinker MBA were stirred and blended thoroughly. Then, the initiator PPS was added. Finally, free-radical polymerisation reaction was conducted to generate the 3D porous structured CNH.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image was taken using JCM-7000 scanning electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). The crystal structure of the samples were determined by X-ray diffractometry (XRD) using a Rigaku D/MAX2500 diffractometer (Rigaku Corporation, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were collected in the attenuated total reflectance (ATR)-FTIR mode via the IN 10TM FTIR Spectrometer (Nicolet, USA). PL spectra were obtained via F-7000 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were obtained via a UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured using Sigma Probe spectrometer (VG Scientific, UK). N2 adsorption text was carried out via a Tri-Star II surface area and porosity analyzer (Micromeritics, USA). Time-resolved PL spectra were recorded via an FL3-111 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horida, Japan). Detailed electrochemical measurement experiments were described in Supplementary material.

The morphology of the prepared samples was observed. CNs were longer fiber-like rods with a smooth surface (Fig. 2 a), and the CDs were spherical with a mean average diameter size of 3.0 nm (Fig. S1). In addition, the rod-like CNs–CDs showed a coarse surface (Fig. 2b), suggesting that the CDs were conjugated onto the surface of the CNs [17]. Fig. S2 shows urchin-like α-FeOOH, which is consistent with previous studies [15]. Furthermore, SEM image of CNH-3 indicated the successful construction of a 3D porous structure (Fig. 2c). In the CNH-3, the incorporation of α-FeOOH was confirmed by EDS spectra (Fig. S3) and a magnified SEM image (Fig. S4). High-resolution TEM image (Fig. S5) of CNH-3 provided insights of nanocrystals with lattice spacing of 0.21 nm and 0.42 nm, agreeing well with the (100) lattice plane of CDs and the (110) lattice plane of α-FeOOH, respectively [18]. The specific surface areas of the CNs, CNs–CDs and CNH-3 were estimated using the nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm (Fig. S6). The specific surface area of CNs-CDs (7.0232 m2/g) was close to that of CNs (6.6963 m2/g), suggesting that the covalent reaction between CNs and CDs had less effect on the specific surface area of CNs [19]. CNH-3 had an improved specific surface area (37.8804 m2/g) because of the construction of a 3D porous structure [20]. In addition, Fig. S7 shows N2 adsorption–desorption curves of CNH-1, CNH-2, CNH-3 and CNH-4. It was found that CNH-3 had the largest specific surface area among these prepared hydrogels.

Fig. 2d shows XRD patterns of CNs, CNs–CDs, α-FeOOH and CNH-3. In the cases of CNs and CNs–CDs, three same bands were observed at 22.7° ((200)) plane), 16.2° ((110)) plane) and 14.9° ((1
1
¯
0)) plane), confirming that the original crystal structure of CNs had been preserved [21]. The XRD pattern of α-FeOOH showed eight bands at 2θ = 64.1°, 61.6°, 59.2°, 53.2°, 41.2°, 36.6°, 33.2° and 21.2°, corresponding to (061), (002), (151), (221), (140), (111), (130) and (110) planes, respectively [22]. Furthermore, the main bands of CNH-3 fitted well with those of α-FeOOH and CNs–CDs, confirming the existence of α-FeOOH and CNs–CDs. In addition, all these obtained hydrogels (CNH-1, CNH-2, CNH-3 and CNH-4) were characterized by XRD (Fig. S8). It was found that these four hydrogels had the diffraction peaks from α-FeOOH and CNs, and with the increasing of α-FeOOH content, the intensity of the XRD peaks corresponding to α-FeOOH gradually increased.

Fig. 2e presents the FTIR spectra of CNs, CNs–CDs, α-FeOOH and CNH-3. In the cases of CNs and CNs–CDs, the band at 1053 cm−1 was assigned to the CO stretching vibration [23]. For CNs–CDs, a new band at 1650 cm−1 corresponding to the –CO–NH– stretching vibration appeared, confirming the covalent grafting of CDs to CNs [17]. The typical spectrum of α-FeOOH powder showed two peaks at 795 and 890 cm−1, corresponding to the in-plane bending vibration because of the presence of a OH group, and the peak at 1132 cm−1, corresponding to the Fe–O vibration [18]. For CNH-3, the abovementioned characteristic bands from α-FeOOH and CNs–CDs were observed, confirming the loading of α-FeOOH and CNs–CDs.

Fig. 2f shows the XPS survey spectra of CNs, CNs–CDs, α-FeOOH and CNH-3. It was found that CNs–CDs contained an extra N element than CNs due to the crosslink of aminomodified CDs to CNs. Meanwhile, CNH-3 obtained C, N, O and Fe elements, confirming the existence of CDs (containing N element) and α-FeOOH (containing Fe element) in the CNH-3. The O1s XPS spectra of α-FeOOH and CNH-3 are shown in Fig. 2g. The two peaks centered at 529.7 and 531.1 eV were associated with Fe–O–Fe and FeOH in α-FeOOH, respectively [24]. Meanwhile, the O1s XPS spectra of CNH-3 exhibited four peaks at 529.9, 531.2, 532.4 and 533.4 eV, which were attributed to Fe–O–Fe, FeOH, CO and CO, respectively. Compared with α-FeOOH, these peaks for Fe–O–Fe and FeOH in CNH-3 shifted toward higher binding energies. In addition, the Fe2p XPS spectrum of α-FeOOH showed two main peaks at 710.6 and 724.0 eV, corresponding to Fe2p3/2 and Fe2p1/2 orbitals of Fe3+, respectively [22] (Fig. 2h). For CNH-3, the corresponding peaks were located at 710.8 and 724.7 eV, showing positive shifts for these two peaks. The decrease in electron density may have caused the increase in the binding energy of XPS peaks. Hence, these positive shifts indicated a decreased electron density on the α-FeOOH in CNH-3. Fig. 2i shows the high-resolution N1s spectra of CNH-3 and CNs–CDs. The N1s spectrum of the CNs–CDs was deconvoluted into three peaks, i.e., 398.7, 399.9 and 401.7 eV, which were assigned to –N=, –NH2 and –NH3 +, respectively [25]. These three peaks had a small shift toward lower binding energy in CNH-3. This change further confirmed the electronic interaction between α-FeOOH and CDs [26].

UV–vis DRS was performed to study the optical properties of these prepared samples. As shown in Fig. 3 a, all three samples had good UV light absorbing abilities. Compared to α-FeOOH, the intensity of CH-3 decreased due to the dilution of α-FeOOH [27]. In addition, CNH-3 had the strongest UV light absorption ability due to the loading of CDs. The bandgap energy (E g) was calculated using the Kubelka–Munk function (inset in Fig. 3a). It was found that the E g value of CNH-3 (2.28 eV) was lower than that of CH-3 (2.29 eV) and α-FeOOH (2.30 eV), confirming the improved photo-absorption performance of CNH-3 [28].

Fig. 3b presents valence band (VB)–XPS valence spectra of α-FeOOH, CH-3 and CNH-3. The VB potential of these three samples was 1.83, 1.81 and 1.67 eV, respectively. According to the relationship between the E g and VB potential [29], the conduction band (CB) potential of the three samples was calculated to be −0.47, −0.48 and −0.61 eV, respectively.

Fig. 3c shows the time-resolved PL spectra of these prepared samples. Both CNH-3 and CH-3 showed double exponential decay characteristics. Moreover, CNH-3 had a shorter fluorescence lifetime (6.05 ns) than CH-3 (6.21 ns), which might be caused by the pathway of charge carriers through the connection between α-FeOOH and CDs [30].

Fig. 3d shows the PL spectra of α-FeOOH powder, CH-3 and CNH-3. All three samples exhibited the same peak at approximately 470 nm, which was assigned to α-FeOOH [18]. Moreover, the CNH-3 had the lowest intensity, indicating the lowest recombination efficiency of photogenerated electron–hole pairs [15,31,32]. These changes may be associated with the construction of a 3D porous structure and the loading of CDs in CNH-3 [33].

Fig. 3e shows the photocurrent data of α-FeOOH powder, CH-3 and CNH-3. CNH-3 had an advantageous photocurrent density, suggesting that the construction of a 3D porous structure and the loading of CDs were favourable to the separation and movement of photogenerated charge [34].

EIS was further used to investigate the charge transfer dynamics. Fig. 3f shows the semicircular Nyquist plots for these three samples. The arc radius was in the order of α-FeOOH powder > CH-3 > CNH-3, confirming the highest charge transfer rate in CNH-3, which was consistent with the photocurrent data [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of adsorption on Cr(VI) removal
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S9 shows the adsorption capacity (Q e) of CNH-3 as a function of pH. As the pH increased from 1 to 6, the Q e first increased and then decreased, and the highest Q e was obtained at pH 2. This change was associated with the ionic states of CNH-3 and Cr(VI). At pH 1, chromic acid (H2CrO4) was the main species. The Q e was low because of the weak electrostatic attraction between the neutral chromic acid and CNH-3. During the pH range from 2 to 6, HCrO4 − and Cr2O7 2− were the predominant species, and an electrostatic force existed. With increasing pH, the protonation degree of CNH-3 was reduced, causing a decrease in adsorption capacity [36]. It was confirmed that the optimum pH for CNH-3 was 2.

Fig. 4 a presents the relationship between equilibrium sorption capacity (Q e) and initial Cr(VI) concentration. The Q e of CNH-3 was larger than that of CH-3 at the same initial Cr(VI) concentration. Hence, the adsorption ability of CNH-3 was enhanced via the loading of CDs. Two isotherm models, i.e., Langmuir (Eq. (1)) and Freundlich models (Eq. (2)), were applied to fit the sorption isotherms [37,38].

where Q max is the theoretical maximum adsorption capacity; C e is the equilibrium concentration of Cr(VI) ions; K L and K F are the Langmuir and Freundlich adsorption constant, respectively; n is the heterogeneity factor; R 2 is the correlation coefficient. The fitting results are illustrated in Fig. 4b and 4c, and the corresponding parameters are listed in Table S1. Based on the R 2 values, the Cr(VI) adsorption by the CNH-3 was more suitable for the Langmuir model, suggesting the monolayer adsorption process [39]. More importantly, the Q max of CNH-3 from the Langmuir model was 115.98 mg/g, which was about 8 times than that of α-FeOOH (14.38 mg/g, as shown in Fig. S10).

Fig. 4d shows the effect of the contact time on the adsorption capacity. The adsorption capacity (Q t) of CNH-3 was larger than that of CH-3 at the same contact time (t), confirming that the loading of CDs accelerated the Cr(VI) adsorption rate. Two kinetics models, i.e., pseudo-first-order (Eq. (3)) and pseudo-second-order (Eq. (4)) models were used to fit the experimental data [37,38].

where Q e is the equilibrium adsorption capacity, and k 1 and k 2 are the pseudo-first-order and the pseudo-second-order rate constants, respectively. The fitting results are illustrated in Fig. 4e and 4f, and the corresponding parameters are listed in Table S2. Based on the R 2 values, Cr(VI) adsorption by the CNH-3 was more suitable for the pseudo-second-order model, suggesting that chemisorption was the rate-controlling step [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergetic effects of adsorption and photocatalysis on Cr(VI) removal
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 a shows Cr(VI) removal in the presence of α-FeOOH powder, CH-3 and CNH-3. During the adsorption process of 60 min, adsorption–desorption equilibrium was established. At an illumination time of 80 min, the removal efficiencies of α-FeOOH, CH-3 and CNH-3 were 18.9 %, 81.0 % and 100.0 %, respectively. The experimental data of these three samples in the photocatalytic process were fitted using the pseudo-first-order model (Fig. 5b). The photocatalytic reduction rate constant (k) of α-FeOOH, CH-3 and CNH-3 was 0.00244, 0.01943 and 0.06380 min−1, respectively. The CNH-3 had the largest k, which was approximately 26 times than that of α-FeOOH. The results indicated that the CH-3 had better Cr(VI) removal performance compared with α-FeOOH due to the construction of a 3D porous structure. The construction of a 3D porous structure increased the specific surface area and provided additional active sites, thereby improving removal efficiency. Meanwhile, the CNH-3 had higher Cr(VI) removal efficiency than CH-3 due to the loading of CDs. The loading of CDs enhanced amino group-induced adsorption for Cr(VI) and promoted photocatalytic properties of α-FeOOH, thereby improving removal efficiency.

Fig. 5c exhibits the influences of pH on Cr(VI) removal. At pH 2, the removal efficiency of CNH-3 was approximately 100 % under the irradiation of 80 min, indicating that NCH-2 had favourable pH scope. The experimental data of CNH-3 at four different pH values in the photocatalytic process were fitted by the pseudo-first-order model (Fig. 5d). The k values of CNH-3 at these five pH (1, 2, 3, 4 and 5) were 0.03548, 0.06380, 0.02962, 0.01983 and 0.01519 min−1, respectively. The following two factors may cause these changes. First, the increase in pH reduced the protonation of CNH-3, which then reduced the Cr(VI) adsorption ability. Second, the increase in pH value decreased the amount of H+, adversely affecting the structural changes from Cr(VI) to Cr(III) in the photocatalysis process [41].

Fig. 5e shows the influences of the α-FeOOH loading amount on Cr(VI) removal. The removal efficiency increased with the increase of α-FeOOH loading amount from 0.05 to 0.15 g. The Cr(VI) removal efficiency of CNH-3 within 80 min was approximately 100 % at an α-FeOOH loading amount of 0.15 g. When the loading amount was increased to 0.20, most of the 3D porous structure of CNH was blocked, limiting the adsorptive and photocatalytic activity. The experimental data of these prepared hydrogels in the photocatalytic process were fitted by the pseudo-first-order model (Fig. 5f). The k values of CNH with different α-FeOOH loading amounts were 0.03452, 0.04905, 0.06380 and 0.02887 min−1, respectively. Among these, CNH-3 with α-FeOOH loading amount of 0.15 g had the largest k. The above removal performance showed that CNH-3 had the optimal loading amount of α-FeOOH [42].

Fig. 5g shows the influences of the initial concentration on Cr(VI) removal. CNH-3 had higher removal efficiency when the initial Cr(VI) concentrations were 10 and 20 mg/L, CNH-3 had higher removal efficiency, which was approximately 100 % at the illumination time of 80 min. The increase in initial concentration was unfavorable for the Cr(VI) removal efficiency. The experimental data of CNH-3 at four different initial concentrations in the photocatalytic process were fitted using the pseudo-first-order model (Fig. 5h). The k of CNH-3 at these four concentrations (10, 20, 30 and 40 mg/L) was 0.09499, 0.06380, 0.02307 and 0.01375 min−1, respectively. These changes were attributed to the saturation of active sites at high concentrations [43].

Reusability is an important criterion for practical applications of an adsorptive photocatalyst. In this study, Cr(VI) removal using CNH-3 was investigated for five cycles. Before the next cycle of adsorption and photocatalysis, the CNH-3 sample (0.10 g) was first soaked in a NaOH solution (100 mL, 0.1 M) for 3 h. Subsequently, the CNH-3 sample was washed with deionised water. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the removal efficiency of CNH-3 exhibits no significant decrease after five cycles. Additionally, the structural integrity of CNH-3 was evidenced by photograph, SEM, FTIR and XRD analyses. On the basis of the photograph (Fig. S11) and SEM image (Fig. S12), the structural morphology of CNH-3 after five cycles was very close to that of the as-synthesized CNH-3. The CNH-3 still retained a 3D porous structure. In addition, the chemical groups of CNH-3 after five cycles were similar to that of the original CNH-3, as evidenced by FTIR spectra [44] (Fig. 6b). More importantly, the crystal structure of CNH-3 was well preserved after five adsorption–desorption cycles (Fig. 6c). These results confirmed that CNH-3 obtained good stability and durability after multiple recycling experiments.

It is essential to determine the CDs-enhanced synergistic removal mechanism of adsorption and photocatalysis. Firstly, FTIR and XPS studies were conducted to understand the CDs-enhanced adsorption mechanism.

Fig. 7 a presents the FTIR spectra of CNH-3 before and after Cr(VI) adsorption. The NH and OH stretching vibrations were observed at 3339 cm−1, and the –COO– stretching vibration was observed at 1560 cm−1 [45]. After adsorption, these two bands decreased, and one new band at 902 cm−1 associated with chromium species appeared [46]. It was confirmed that the amino group participated in Cr(VI) sorption.

XPS was also used to analyse the CDs-enhanced adsorption mechanism. Fig. 7b shows the total XPS survey scans of CNH-3. After sorption, two bands of Cr2p (579.8 and 589.1 eV) appeared, confirming chromium sorption. Moreover, Fig. 7c presents the high-resolution Cr2p spectrum of CNH-3. Both Cr2p1/2 and Cr2p3/2 bands were divided into two component bands, separately. The two component bands at 579.8 and 577.1 eV were assigned to Cr(VI) and Cr(III) ions, and those at 589.1 and 586.6 eV were also assigned to Cr(VI) and Cr(III) ions [47]. The results showed that some Cr(VI) ions were reduced to Cr(III) ions. The high-resolution O1s spectrum of CNH-3 before Cr(VI) sorption (Fig. 7d) can be separated into four component bands, corresponding to Fe–O–Fe (529.9 eV), FeOH (531.3 eV), CO (532.4 eV) and CO (533.4 eV) [24]. After sorption, these bands shifted to lower binding energies, indicating the electrostatic attraction between –COO– and Cr(III) ions [48]. Fig. 7e presents high-resolution N1s spectra before and after sorption. Before sorption, the N1s band was separated into three component bands at 398.1, 399.8 and 401.3 eV corresponding to –N=, –NH2 and –NH3 +, respectively. After adsorption, an obvious increase in the band associated with –NH3 + was observed. This result confirmed the enhancement of the amino group for Cr(VI) adsorption [25].

To interpret the CDs-enhanced photocatalytic activities, the in-situ XPS technique was used to investigate the electron transfer process on the α-FeOOH and CNH-3 before and after UV illumination [26]. The O1s spectrum of α-FeOOH (Fig. 8 a) was fitted to two component peaks at 529.7 and 531.1 eV corresponding to Fe–O–Fe and FeOH, respectively. The Fe2p spectrum of α-FeOOH (Fig. 8b) included two component peaks at 710.6 and 724.0 eV, corresponding to Fe2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 orbitals of Fe3+, respectively. In the case of α-FeOOH, no obvious shift of these component peaks was observed before and after UV illumination. However, in the O1s spectrum of CNH-3 (Fig. 8c), after UV irradiation, the two peaks corresponding to Fe–O–Fe and Fe–OH shifted to a high binding energy, indicating the transfer of photogenerated electron. In addition, in the Fe2p spectrum of CNH-3 (Fig. 8d), all the Fe2p component peaks shifted to higher binding energy, further demonstrating that the migration of photogenerated electron from the α-FeOOH to the CDs acted as electron acceptors [18].

We examined the effects of four free-radical trapping agents, including ethanol, isopropanol (IPA), benzoquinone (BQ) and AgNO3 acting as the holes (h+), hydroxyl radicals (OH), superoxide anion (O2 −) and electron (e−) scavengers (Fig. 9 a). In the presence of ethanol, IPA, BQ, AgNO3 and no scavenger, k was 0.09010, 0.05866, 0.02017, 0.01115 and 0.06380 min−1, respectively (Fig. 9b). Hence, e− and O2 − were the main reactive species for CNH-3. Furthermore, EPR spin-trapping using 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) and TEMPO measurements was performed to confirm the functions of the radicals. DMPO-O2 − and TEMPO-h+ signals were observed under UV irradiation, and the subsequent peak intensities were stable (Fig. 9c and d). On the other hand, the DMPO-OH signal was not found (Fig. 9e), because VB holes of CNH-3 were not positive enough to generate OH radicals as E(OH/OH−) was + 1.99 eV [32]. Therefore, both O2 − radical and e− participated in the Cr (VI) photoreduction [49]. In the case of CNH-3, the loading of CDs helped produce more e− and O2 −, because CDs could act as electron acceptors [50]. This may be one reason why CDs could enhance the photocatalytic activity of CNH-3.

In this study, we proposed a method for synthesising a novel CNH using CNs, CDs and α-FeOOH, with the CDs-enhanced synergetic effect between adsorption and photocatalysis, for removing Cr(VI). The CNH was characterized with respect to its morphological features and chemical composition, and then the construction of 3D porous structure and the loading of amino-modified CDs were confirmed. The decreased PL intensity, increased photocurrent and small circular arc radius of EIS plots of CNH reflected the effective separation of the photoexcited electrons and holes. A series of adsorption and photocatalysis experiments showed the superior adsorption-photocatalytic performance of CNH, as demonstrated by the large adsorption capacity and high kinetic constant in the photocatalytic process of 115.98 mg/g and 0.06380, respectively, which is about 8 times and 26 times as high as that of α-FeOOH powder. This superior performance was due to the mechanism of CDs-enhanced adsorption-photocatalysis synergy. This study not only provided a novel strategy for preparing green and efficient bifunctional materials for high-efficient Cr(VI) removal but also proposed a corresponding novel mechanism of CDs-enhanced adsorption-photocatalysis synergy which was helpful for designing and optimizing multi-functional materials.
